texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane wetting is a limitation to mass transfer in industrial Hollow-Fiber Membrane Modules (HFMM). The influence of transmembrane pressure on wetting is investigated through both experimental and numerical works. On the one hand, experiments for CO2 absorption in softened water are carried out at pilot scale with Liqui-Cel Ò Extra-Flow modules operating in a cross-flow mode. After several hours of operation (50-300 min), the membrane partially wetted mode is established at steady state and decreases the absorbed CO2 flowrates up to 10.5% compared to membrane dry mode. Interestingly, CO2 absorption is found to be independent on different transmembrane pressure (0.5 and 2.5 bar), both in the dry and partially wetted mode. On the other hand, a 2D predictive tool is developed to help in the design of HFMM gas absorption systems. The model depicts the complex hydrodynamics in commercial cross-flow modules. The liquid in the shellside is flowing through the fiber bundle considered as a porous medium. The liquid mass-transfer coefficient is non-uniform over the membrane module and is estimated at a local scale. The parameters from the empirical Chilton-Colburn correlation are optimized with experimental results in the dry mode through a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm. The simulation results are in good agreement with the experiments ( � 13/+17%). In the partially wetted mode at steady state, wetting is then described by considering a log-normal pore size distribution and the Laplace-Young equation. The transmembrane pressure decreases the simulated absorbed CO2 flowrate. By comparing to experimental data, the numerical relative error is increasing with transmembrane pressure. Therefore, a suggestion is made to adapt the description of wetting in HFMM gas-liquid models. Ó 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane is an interface for mass transfer. It can be passive (without selectivity) or contain a thin selective layer depending on the application. However, the presence of the membrane introduces an additional resistance to mass transfer. Regarding gas-liquid applications, this membrane resistance is often negligible compared to the diffusion layer resistance in the liquid phase while operating in the non-wetted mode. However, (Kreulen et al., 1993) stated that mass transfer resistance increases in the wetted mode when the membrane pores are filled with liquid. This phenomenon was studied by Mavroudi et al. (2006) for CO2 absorption in water: the permanent regime in the wetted mode was established after three hours of operation and the total mass transfer resistance increased by 21-53% compared to the dry mode. These observations require further investigation in a process optimization perspective.

texts.orig: The wetting phenomenon is described as dependent on many parameters: pore size, surface tension, contact angle and operating transmembrane pressure. The choice of the solvent and the membrane material is of the utmost importance to avoid membrane wetting (Mosadegh-Sedghi et al., 2014). The influence of most of these parameters have been investigated. Lv et al. (2010) noticed a variation up to 20-30 ° in the contact angle when the hollow fiber was immersed in different solvents. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) observations confirmed the apparition of structure modifications in the membrane after wetting. Bougie and Iliuta (2013) performed breakthrough pressure measurements and observed a decrease in the maximal pore size after wetting.

Both models considered uniform wetting by progression of the gas-liquid interface through the membrane and demonstrated that wetting could significantly decrease removal efficiency. Several studies reported that the membrane wetting along the length of the fiber was not constant due to liquid pressure drop and the wetting ratio was therefore considered as position dependent in the model (Albarracin Zaidiza et al., 2016; Goyal et al., 2015; Rongwong et al., 2015). Recently, Cui et al. (2015) proposed a dynamic model to describe membrane wetting, taking into account the pressure drop in the liquid phase along the contactor. The predictions captured well the slow timedependence of wetting phenomenon. DEA concentration increase was found to decrease the wetting ratio while liquid temperature and velocity were found to increase it.

texts.orig: At the same time, the studies developing new membrane materials for CO2 capture focused on the impact of membrane wetting on the absorbed flowrate. Polypropylene (PP) has a lower production cost but a lower hydrophobicity. Tang et al. (2016) developed polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membranes which did not show a significant decrease in the performances. Gomez-Coma et al. (2016) developed a polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane filled with liquid to avoid wetting issues. The immobilization within the pores of a liquid such as an ionic one with a high diffusion coefficient and a high solubility level for CO2 demonstrated to increase the CO2 permeation up to 43%. Recently, Fashandi et al. (2016) found out that poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) can provide CO2 absorption performances comparable to the common hydrophobic polymers PVDF, PTFE and PP. Its good processability and cost effectiveness make it a promising candidate for future developments. Ceramic membranes could offer more reliability and higher thermal stability. The kaolin-alumina membrane produced by Abdulhameed et al. (2017) was grafted with fluoroalkylsilane (FAS) to develop surface hydrophobicity and limit wetting. It demonstrated a CO2 absorption flowrate of 0 : 18 mol m � 2 s � 1 at a distilled water flowrate of 100 mL min � 1 . With a similar surface modification process on porous alumina membranes, Lee and Park (2016) observed a decrease of 15% in the absorbed CO2 flux into MEA solution only after 72 h of operation. This observation could not directly be attributed to wetting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of the present work is to experimentally study the influence of transmembrane pressure on partial wetting under moderate gas pressure (3 : 0 and 5 : 0 bar g). These experimental data on CO2 absorption in water further enrich the data available in the literature. Simultaneously, a simulation tool is developed to reflect the cross-flow geometry of the Liqui-Cel Ò modules. For industrialization purposes, this model will predict the local mass transfer coefficients in order to be able to scale-up to a larger HFMM with a similar geometry. The hydrodynamic model of the liquid flow in the shell takes the diverting baffle into account and is developed by considering the fiber bundle as a porous medium. This model is validated with experimental data in the nonwetted mode. Model and experimental results are then compared in the partially wetted mode.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to avoid phase mixing, liquid phase pressure is kept higher than gas phase pressure. The hydrophobic properties of the membrane prevent water from flowing across the membrane. Nonetheless, a limitation to mass transfer has been reported in the literature (Kreulen et al., 1993; Mavroudi et al., 2006).

texts.orig: It appears that wetting is strongly dependent on the solvent nature and the membrane features, as well as on the operating conditions. Besides some of these parameters may vary over the length of the membrane module. A non-uniformity in the pore size distribution along the fibers would lead to a more intense wetting where pores are larger (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999). Also, a pressure drop might occur on the liquid side. If the module is operated countercurrently, higher transmembrane pressures and a subsequent more important wetting could be observed (Dindore et al., 2004). In the previous equations, these effects are not taken into account since no significant pressure drops were experimentally measured, neither on the liquid shellside, nor on the gas lumenside.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The set-up was designed to monitor the absorption of CO2 in softened water in different operating conditions (flowrates, pressures) and to test the influence of transmembrane pressure on membrane wetting. The set-up is detailed in Fig. 3. Pure CO2 (>99.95%, Alphagaz 1 Air Liquide) was used as a feed gas, while softened water was flowing counter-currently. The inlet gas flowrate was regulated by a mass flow controller (Bronkhorst In-Flow CTA). Another mass flow controller (Bronkhorst LowD P-Flow) measures the outlet gas flowrate and regulates the gas pressure in the HFMM. The water pump is controlled by a vortex flowmeter

texts.orig: The reproducibility of the experimental results was assessed by comparison of two duplicates of a control experiment. At the beginning and at the end of the campaign, a new experiment was performed under control operating conditions after overnight membrane drying. Both duplicate and control essays resulted in the same flow profiles. There was indeed only a negligible decrease in the absorbed flowrate at the end of the campaign within the precision range of the gas flowmeters (5 : 56 -10 � 6 N m 3 = s for the second duplicate accounting for 1.0% of the inlet flowrate).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this experimental set-up, the operating conditions (flowrates and pressures) can be varied in the ranges presented in Table 3. In a first set of experiments, CO2 absorption was studied at a constant liquid pressure (5 : 5 bar g) and different gas pressures (3 and 5 bar g). In a second set of experiments, the influence of transmembrane pressure was investigated at 3 bar g in the gas lumenside and different liquid pressures (3 : 5 and 5 : 5 bar g). Since the liquid flowrate is driving gas absorption in membrane contactors (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999; Karoor and Sirkar, 1993; McLeod et al., 2013), three liquid flowrates were studied (1.67-3.33-5.00 -10 � 5 m 3 /s). The gas flowrate was set by default at 19 : 4 -10 � 5 N m 3 = s and increased by adding another mass flowmeter in parallel, increasing uncertainties at the same time from  0 : 2 -10 � 5 N m 3 = s to  0 : 4 -10 � 5 N m 3 = s.

texts.orig: The equation system was implemented and solved using the open source CFD framework OpenFOAM. A sequential approach with an implicit formulation and second order numerical schemes was used. The mesh convergence was achieved using a 9000 square cells mesh. Solver tolerance was set below 10 � 4 . Domain imbalance was verified to be below 10 � 6 . The runtime is about 15 min on a single thread (Intel Core i7-4910 MQ Haswell at 2.90 MHz, 16 Go DDR3 1600 MHz).

texts.orig: The model parameters were determined using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) method. This method was originally based on the observation of animal groups behavior (Eberhart et al., 1995; Reynolds, 1987). Its mathematical relevance has been especially shown in cases requiring the optimization of numerous parameters (Andras, 2012). This method has already been successful to solve various problems (building cost optimization, solar power plant design, . . . ) (Banks et al., 2008; Farges, 2014), including some with highly discontinuous objective functions (Wetter and Wright, 2004). The PSO algorithm is run until the parameters are determined with two significant digits.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results of CO2 absorption in the wet regime at steady state for two transmembrane pressures ð 0 : 5 and 2 : 5 bar g Þ were compared in (Fig. 10) with the data obtained previously in the dry regime (Fig. 8). Firstly, as found by Mavroudi et al. (2006), the absorption flowrates are slightly lower in the wet regime than in the dry one for the higher liquid flowrates. Similarly to the dry regime where the absorption flowrates were the same for both transmembrane pressures (difference lower than 5%), the differences between absorption flowrates at the same liquid flowrate are negligible (less than 5%). The comparison of Figs. 8 and 10 shows that the trends of CO2 absorption in both regimes are very similar. The transmembrane pressure has therefore little influence on CO2 absorption; this experimental result is surprising as this operating parameter was expected to have an influence on the critical pore radius (Eq. (4)) and subsequently on membrane wetting.

texts.orig: Modifications in the model of the membrane mass transfer in partial wetting mode could bring an explanation. In the literature as well as in this study, the membrane porosity is described by cylindrical non-interconnected pores. Wetting implies that the larger pores are fully wetted while smaller pores are still dry. The resistances of these two categories of pores have been considered in series so far in the literature and in the present model (Eq. (15)).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane wetting was experimentally investigated through CO2 absorption in softened water. It was found to decrease the absorbed CO2 flux up to 10.5%. The results suggest that the transmembrane pressure (between 0 : 5 and 2 : 5 bar g) has no impact on the mass transfer limitation due to partial wetting. In parallel, a model was developed to describe gas-liquid mass transfer. This model aims at providing a scale-up predictive tool for multicomponent absorption. Therefore, the specific cross-flow hydrodynamics of commercial Liqui-Cel Ò Extra-Flow modules is modeled at a local scale, by considering the fiber bundle as a porous medium.
texts.text: Membrane wetting was experimentally investigated through CO2 absorption in softened water. It was found to decrease the absorbed CO2 flux up to 10.5%. The results suggest that the transmembrane pressure (between 0 : 5 and 2 : 5 bar g) has no impact on the mass transfer limitation due to partial wetting. In parallel, a model was developed to describe gas-liquid mass transfer. This model aims at providing a scale-up predictive tool for multicomponent absorption. Therefore, the specific cross-flow hydrodynamics of commercial Liqui-Cel Ò Extra-Flow modules is modeled at a local scale, by considering the fiber bundle as a porous medium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Numerical results are in good agreement with experimental ones ( � 13/+17% error) when membrane is operated in the dry mode. The liquid mass transfer coefficient is estimated by a Chilton-Colburn correlation, optimized on experimental data with a PSO algorithm. In the wet mode, the membrane pore size distribution is considered. The pores which size is above the LaplaceYoung critical radius are considered as totally wetted. Then, the membrane mass transfer coefficient is estimated from the wetting ratio. The relative error between simulated and experimental results increases with the transmembrane pressure up to 83%. Therefore, the penetration of water in the membrane is not properly described by the Laplace-Young equation. Additional local measurements and complementary tests would strengthen the newly proposed modelling description of partial wetting (Eq. (33)).

Therefore, the penetration of water in the membrane is not properly described by the Laplace-Young equation. Additional local measurements and complementary tests would strengthen the newly proposed modelling description of partial wetting (Eq. (33)).
